sentence: Subjective:
The patient is a 30-year-old male who presents with a 2-year history of noticing difficulty breathing, feeling short of breath, and experiencing sudden mid-shortness of breath without a clear reason.
He denies any other symptoms such as fever, nausea, vomiting, rash, or lip/throat swelling.
He reports using an albuterol inhaler prescribed by urgent care for similar symptoms in the past.
He denies any history of allergies, heavy drinking, or recent travels.
Past medical history:
The patient has a past medical history of buying a plot of land and being a hunter, with a full refrigerator at home filled with venison.
Review of systems:
The patient denies any symptoms in other organs that are relevant to his initial presentation.
Current medications:
The patient is currently using an albuterol inhaler as prescribed by urgent care.
Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's pulse is okay, and his pulse ox is normal.
Physical exam: The patient does not appear to be in any distress and does not have a rash, angioedema, or audible stridor.
There is faint expiratory wheezing bilaterally in all lung fields.
Test Results:
The patient had a normal chest x-ray and pulmonary function test.
Assessment / Problem List:
The patient has newly diagnosed allergic asthma.
The patient has asthma; active: confirmed.
Plan:
I prescribed albuterol inhaler as previously prescribed by urgent care, and I also prescribed singulair 10mg once daily to help decrease the occurrences of asthma symptoms.
I will proceed with allergy testing, starting with skin testing, to identify potential triggers.
I want to see the patient next week to schedule the skin testing.
Follow-up:
I want to see the patient next week to schedule the skin testing.
